text.fullText: February 17, 2026 RE: Docket ID No. EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 requires that any applicant for a Federal license or permit to conduct any activity which may result in any discharge into the navigable waters of the United States, shall provide the licensing or permitting agency a water quality certification from the State agency with jurisdiction over water pollution control. In Puerto Rico, such agency is the Puerto Rico Department of Natural and Environmental Resources (DNER) pursuant to the provisions of Act No. 416- 2004, as amended, Puerto Rico Public Environmental Policy Act and after enactment of the “Reorganizational Plan of the Department of Natural and Environmental Resources of 2018”, Act No. 171- 2018. Herein the DNER submits its comments regarding the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) intention to Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Definitions • In the text of the proposed revisions to the rule the term “project proponent” is substituted by “applicant” nevertheless, “applicant” is not defined. The definition of “project proponent” is proposed to be deleted. The term “applicant” should be defined in the rule. This is relevant particularly when the federal agency is proposing a general permit like the Nationwide Permits in the case of the Corps of Engineers or the general NPDES permits for stormwater discharges in the case of EPA. The rule should expressly address the procedures for those instances in which the federal agency proposes a general permit. • The proposed revision of the definition of “water quality requirements” deleted the reference to “…any federal and state or tribal laws or regulations implementing those sections”. This reference is important and necessary because those are legal frameworks that establish the procedures, for the determination of compliance with the requirements of the CWA’s sections identified in this definition, for example the state Water Quality Standards Regulation. Certification Request (Part 121.5) Part 121.5 should include provisions to request the applicant to comply with any requirement established in the applicable State laws and regulations. For example, in the case of Puerto Rico, the request for certification should include all the necessary information required by the Environmental Public Policy Act of Puerto Rico and /or the Puerto Rico Water Quality Standards Regulation that allow the Department of Natural and Environmental Resources (DNER) as the certifying authority, to verify compliance with the Antidegration Policy or complete any procedure for the translation of water quality standards into effluent limitations using Waste Load Allocations, Mixing Zone or any other water quality standard implementation procedure or provision included in the abovementioned law and regulation. These will allow the DNER to determine compliance with the applicable water quality requirements included in CWA sections 301, 302, 303 and 307. Modification to a grant of certification (Part 121.10) The proposed rule regarding modifications requiring the certifying authority to obtain the applicant's agreement on the language of the modification does not make sense from a regulatory agency or governmental authority point of view and responsibilities. The scope of the applicant's involvement in a determination regarding modifications to an issued certification must be defined, in such a way that it is clear that when the state and the applicant do not agree on the proposed modification, the final decision rests within the certifying agency in the exercise of its experience, and statutory or ministerial functions, when such a decision is necessary to ensure compliance with water quality requirements. The CWA’s objective is “to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation's waters…”. Limiting the authority and responsibility of the state agencies to the full agreement of the applicant is a risk for sustainable and responsible economic development, and the right of every citizen to the well-being, use, and enjoyment of the waters of the United States.
